title: A note on human-scale robotics as an art project with civic consequences
summary: A personal essay about human-scale robotics, and the longer argument hidden inside the gap between wonder and maintenance.
topic: human-scale robotics as an art project with civic consequences
body: My notes on human-scale robotics began with a small irritation in April 2019. The easy agreement around the big story felt like a warning, not a comfort. Quick agreement often means someone has already done the trimming required to make the thought portable. In this case the omitted piece was the gap between wonder and maintenance. The abstract version vanishes as soon as you track who has to maintain the thing, who bears its failures, and who gains from the trust it extracts.

Human-scale robotics appealed to me because it sat right on the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering story is easy to tell because it arrives with visible pieces and measurable claims. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Each tool contains a small politics of the user and the surrounding world, whether it announces that politics or not. The design tends to confess those assumptions to anyone willing to look closely. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about the quiet mood and dependency structure that settles in around the system.

The most important distance was the one between the prototype environment and the wider public environment. In a prototype, people understand that the thing is provisional. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. There is a big difference between testing on willing enthusiasts and asking ordinary people to inherit the result. It is another thing entirely to fold that same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the surface looks modern. The gap between wonder and maintenance becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

My taste for the subject stayed mostly intact, but my threshold for abstraction fell sharply. After writing “On solar abundance as a rehearsal for a different kind of city,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not expect it to last. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But we should stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and novelty gets marketed as destiny. Human-scale robotics seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. What becomes durable is often less the tool than the pattern of relationship it makes routine.

A final note to myself from April 2019: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. At this point the burden a tool places on nearby people matters more to me than the abstract future it points toward. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: On solar abundance as a rehearsal for a different kind of city
